@uiri Why does it matter if it breaks the test suite if the test suite is incorrect? I've (and you've) mentioned @BurntSushi in both posts, and there are issues opened (https://github.com/BurntSushi/toml-test/issues/18) about the problem. In any case, `pytoml` does the right thing here, and I'm fairly certain (though I haven't tried) that these changes will pass a fork of @BurntSushi's test suite: https://github.com/avakar/toml-test/

You need 2 backslash like this `test = "\\"one\\""`, the escape is misleading sometimes.

But it may be a bug when dealing with escape, no exception but unexpected result:

```python
import json
import toml

text1 = '{"test": "\\"one\\""}'
text2 = 'test = "\\"one\\""'

print(json.loads(text1))  # output: {'test': '"one"'}
print(toml.loads(text2))  # output: {'test': 'n'}
```
No problem!

The primary difficulty with ``%>`` is the following case:
```
<%
a = "this is python"
%>
```
In order to parse the line ``a = "this is python"``, Plim would have to use Python AST, because it would have to find ``%>`` somewhere after ``<%`` and omit cases like:
```
<%
a = "%>"
%>
```
and
```
<%
a = "%>"%><%
b = "another block"
%>
```

Hi @GriMel,

I faced this limitation too so I saw that flower holds - using the celery EventsState mechanism - a LRUCache of **10000** tasks by default (c.f celery/events/state.py).

In order to raise it you will have to create a flowerconfig.py file with a "**max_tasks**" entry.

Caution if you want to do it :

- if you use a persistent shelve file to save the task set ("persistent" and "db" entries in the same file), it saves the state as is with the default "10000" limitation at the end of the program

- for a large tasks set, the search using the API is in O(n) and it can take seconds. I'm trying to optimize it using ordering and partitioning with itertools but it does not help very much

@njsmith , I get your point. I agree, for LIFO and FIFO we don't have much of a choice right now, as it's hard to tell which IO came first, and it's also hard to tell whether IO should come before sleeping tasks. At least. 

I somewhat disagree with your statement about priority queue though. If we use something like a binary heap, we can guarantee that the task with a higher priority will be scheduled before any other task with lower priority, even if we push it to the ready queue after other tasks. And note, that this property will be preserved even with the current event loop implementation, where we schedule IO and timers "in batches".

According to @MasterOdin earlier in the thread, we can identify phantoms by the fact that they are published by Project Gutenberg and they have no title nor author. The following query implements this search:

```py
from gutenberg.acquire.metadata import load_metadata

# warning: this is slow, takes minutes to run on my machine
phantom_query = load_metadata().query('''
SELECT ?uri
WHERE {
  ?uri
  <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#type>
  <http://www.gutenberg.org/2009/pgterms/ebook>.

  ?uri
  <http://purl.org/dc/terms/publisher>
  "Project Gutenberg".

  FILTER(NOT EXISTS { ?uri <http://purl.org/dc/terms/creator> ?creator })
  FILTER(NOT EXISTS { ?uri <http://purl.org/dc/terms/title> ?title }) }
''')

phantom_urls = frozenset(result['uri'].toPython() for result in phantom_query)

with open('phantom_urls.txt', 'w') as fobj:
    fobj.write('\n'.join(sorted(phantom_urls)))
```

Let's verify that all of these ebooks don't exist using a quick bash script:

```sh
while read phantom_url; do
  curl --silent "${phantom_url}" | grep -q '<title>404 Not Found</title>' || echo "${phantom_url} did not 404";
done < 'phantom_urls.txt'
```

Turns out that we have some false-positives in the query: valid books without authors and titles!
- http://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/50624 did not 404
- http://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/50625 did not 404
- http://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/51307 did not 404
- http://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/51950 did not 404

> Where does the previous version come from?

The previous version is a result of the same training process at an earlier time _t0_ (say, 9:00). It would be used to process new samples until another training iteration is completed at time _t1_ (say, 9:01). At _t1_, the version from _t0_ can be ditched, and the version from _t1_ can be used until yet another iteration is completed at _t2_ (say, 9:02), and so on.

Anyway, as discussed already, I can handle this by creating another thread for the training, and passing trained net objects (e.g. using `dlib::pipe`, which looks convenient, although I haven't used it yet) from that thread to where the net's `operator()` is called. Shouldn't be a problem, I think.

> Is that why you were asking about in memory serialization in the other topic? To create a copy? You should be able to use the normal = operator to make copies of a net.

No, the other topic is not related to this one. In that other topic, I just want to pass the trained network over the wire to another node (computer), where it will be used to process new data. Alternatively, I might want to save the serialized network to a database, or embed it in some other serialization. In all these cases I can first serialize to file (as is done in the examples), and then read the file -- and indeed that is exactly what I was doing first. But surely it is better to avoid such a file operation, when it is not really needed.

**get_face_chip_details**  internals are searching parameters for affine-transform that will map your original image into chip image. if you will look inside, you will find that this function does not use all landmarks from face - only most important one

Helen dataset has 194 face landmarks, and this landmarks looks like extension to dlib's 68 landmarks. So you can find corresponding points from 68-set and 194-set, select them from 194-detected points, produce new **full_object_detection** that will have 68 points selected from 194 detected ones and use this **full_object_detection** instance to **get_face_chip_details**

This is simple and straightforward way. It will have same precision as the full mapping of points - don't be afraid to miss something here

You can also build new **get_face_chip_details** function that will use all landmarks - but is on you :)

Back to your first questions, this is how the rule works:
1. `{'t': 'purchase', 'location': 'US'}` is asserted 
2. It matches the alpha nodes for `first` and `second` expressions
3. It is stored twice in the beta node that joins `first` and `second` expressions (once for every alpha match)
4. No action is scheduled at this point as first and second don't satisfy the `location` condition
5.  `{'t': 'purchase', 'location': 'CA'}` is asserted
6. It matches the alpha nodes for `first` and `second` expressions
7. It is stored twice in the beta node that joins `first` and `second` expressions 
8. At this point two pairs of facts satisfy the `location` condition
9. The frames corresponding to the two matches are added to the action queue
10. The client picks up the last action from the queue and dispatches it
11. The action is successful (Fraud detected -> US, CA), the action is removed from the queue
12. The client picks up the last action from the queue and dispatches it
13. The action is successful (Fraud detected -> CA, US), the action is removed from the queue

Anyway, you are doing a great job but I have totally forgotten to mention this in the README:
* The work is done in the dev branch and the PyPI version is pushed from a master branch.
* Therefore, master pip/master branch is severely outdated at the moment.

In the dev branch I implemented a recursive structure but there is no limit on a depth of recursion as it is using generators to avoid hitting this limit. A tree of type checking nodes (each node being a generator) is assigned for every parameter definition. The incoming argument is then passed to its own tree when a function is invoked. Whenever one of them fails, an exception is raised. In addition, it deals with a concept of Type variables by generating a single tree for every unique TypeVar (if any type T is used in more than one parameter, they will all point to the same tree) which has a special property of remembering the exact type passed to it last time. It will raise an exception if a different type is passed at the later stage.

In addition, I thought of evaluating contents of sequence structures lazily, i.e. the sequence is wrapped in a proxy object which will trigger type check on the content only when such content is accessed. In addition, it might be a good idea to check the first available element of every sequence before wrapping it in a proxy. Therefore, if the first one fails, there is no need for any extra overhead associated with the proxy object.

However, I have not yet implemented any special handling for the sequence, dictionary or callable type checks yet. I had enough time to work on this only during some of the major holidays because of my work. Please help me if you can.

I am especially concerned about checking the signature of callable objects. Honestly, I think the only realistic option would be to wrap it in a proxy object with an expected signature but it will prevent a detection of signature mismatch at the moment of passing such object into the function. As you can see, I do not have a working solution for this yet and hence I am very open to suggestions about how to make it better.

Thanks for all the work you did!

You included both 32-bit and 64-bit versions of the AutoIt DLL, but your test of whether to use the 64-bit version (near the top of `autoit.py`) is wrong. The `PROGRAMFILES` environment variable is always `C:\Program Files`, no matter the bitness of Windows. I don't think it's important to check the bitness of Windows anyway, because you shouldn't ever have a 64-bit Python on a 32-bit Windows. Therefore, once you have determined that you have a 64-bit Python, you might as well just go ahead and use the 64-bit DLL.

The following lines in `autoit.py`:

    if "(x86)" in os.environ['PROGRAMFILES'] and bit == "64bit":
        # if 64bit version of python within 64bit version of Windows,
        # load AutoItX3_x64.dll
        dll = "AutoItX3_x64.dll"

should be

    if bit == "64bit":
        # if 64bit version of python, load AutoItX3_x64.dll
        dll = "AutoItX3_x64.dll"

That will at least get the package to import properly. When I tried the simple script in the PyAutoIt README on my 64-bit Python 2.7, the `control_click()` failed, but that's a separate issue.

Due to the asynchronous nature of the problem, you cannot reliably ensure it won't happen. There are mitigations you can try but they all come with a cost.

One thing would be abruptly killing the `Pool`.

```python
pool = ProcessPool()
completed, pending = concurrent.futures.wait(futures, timeout=GLOBAL_TIMEOUT)

if pending:
    # the pool becomes unusable from now on, create a new one
    pool.stop()
    pool.join()  
```

My bet is you'll still see some message printed.

Another would be to signal to the function itself to give up. This is ugly, resource expensive and deadlock prone so I'd really recommend against.

```python
from multiprocessing import Event

def worker(uid, cancelled_event):
    # if the Event is set, it means the task has been cancelled, give up
    if cancelled_event.is_set():  # 
        return

    ...

def callback(future):
    """If the future was cancelled, we signal to the child process not to pick the job up."""
    if future.cancelled():
        future.cancelled_event.set()

futures = []
for value in range(JOBS):    
    cancelled_event = Event()  # we use a process Event to signal whether the future was cancelled, this is heavy
    future = pool.schedule(worker, args=(value, event)) 
    future.cancelled_event = cancelled_event  
    future.add_done_callback(callback)

completed, pending = concurrent.futures.wait(futures, timeout=GLOBAL_TIMEOUT)
for future in pending:
    future.cancel()
```

Something to keep in mind is that all callbacks are processed in the same thread (actually two, one for suppressing callbacks, and one for non-suppressing, but in this case you are using only non-suppressing). This means that any callback that blocks waiting for a condition will delay the execution of the next callbacks, which may cause unexpected effects.

Try `call_later(wait_enter_game)` from the callback, instead of calling it directly. This will spawn a new thread to wait for the condition.

There's a few ways Crochet can solve this:
1. Have `setup()` notice that you've forked since last time it was called, in which case it'll actually do setup instead of being a no-op. This still requires `setup()` be called in all processes, but makes it more robust.
2. Have a post-fork hook that reinitializes Crochet automatically. Transparent to users, but also tricky to do right (and e.g. slows down launching subprocesses).
3. Every Crochet call that relies on initialization detects if a fork happened, and if it has re-initializes Crochet. Slows down crochet calls but maybe not significantly.

Ok, here we go.

Option 1: Implement your own `getUpdates()` loop. This way, you can access any variables you need. Just call `getUpdates()` with the proper offset before rebooting.

Option 2: Added the following code segments to your diceyclock example. The main points being:

- On receiving the `/reboot` command, you don't reboot right away. Instead, just remember the *need* to reboot.
- The actual rebooting is done after the next `getUpdates()`. I modify the bot's `getUpdates()` method to include a reboot check.

Given that you are following the diceyclock example, I think this solution is most suitable, involving the least amount of changes.

```python
to_reboot = False

def check_reboot(getUpdates):
    # Add a reboot check and call getUpdates() accordingly
    def r(*args, **kwargs):
        if to_reboot:
            # Eliminate `timeout` parameter so getUpdates() returns immediately.
            del kwargs['timeout']
            rtn = getUpdates(*args, **kwargs)
            subprocess.call('sudo reboot', shell=True)
        else:
            # Normally
            rtn = getUpdates(*args, **kwargs)

        return rtn
    return r

# Modify the getUpdates() method to include a reboot check
bot.getUpdates = check_reboot(bot.getUpdates)

def handle(msg):
    global to_reboot
    
    if to_reboot:
        # Anything received AFTER the /reboot command but BEFORE the machine
        # goes down will be ignored.
        return

    ......

    if command == '/reboot':
        # Do not reboot here. Just remember the need to reboot.
        # The actual rebooting is done in the modified getUpdates() method.
        to_reboot = True

    ......
```

Option 3: Same as option 2, but use an object-oriented approach:
- subclass the bot
- add a `to_reboot` member variable
- implement a `handle(msg)` method
- override `getUpdates()` method to include a reboot check

Having seen your problem, I have considered building in a way to better and more formally control looping. But since this problem is not common and can be worked around in above ways, I will hold it off for now.

Hope it helps. Thanks for using telepot.

* discover, if the system has issues with:
 * «ghost» RTM_NEWLINK messages that comes after RTM_DELLINK
 * missing master index on the slave RTM_NEWLINK
 * … [the list is open]
* the capabilities objects *must* be unique per system or namespace due to performance issues, it takes time to collect all the info and the info should not be collected at every IPRSocket instantiation
* the capabilities objects *should* be attached not to config, but IPRSocket objects and should properly represent the target system even if the target system is a remote system.

Alternatives:

* The easiest way would be to guess capabilities by the kernel version — but the backports are unpredictable
* Completely drop the command line fallback support, so if the interface type can not be managed via netlink, it will not be managed
  * A variant: maintain two code branches, «compat» with cli support and «master» with netlink support only

The support of the old systems and the kernel 2.6 is required due to RHEL6.5

…

If you have *any* ideas regarding this, pls write it down. I'm not very glad with the current capabilities implementation as well, since it's in some way destructive.

A syscall is used to create netns — `libc.syscall(__NR_setns, …)`.

Obviously, the library uses wrong syscall number. The hotfix (a proper syscall number) will be provided today.

But probably it's a time to think on more generic solution. The issue is that I'mtrying to avoid using gcc: it would require also kernel and other headers. Not all the platforms can provide that. Without gcc, all that we can, is to guess architecture and try to pick the proper syscall value from constants. So if you have any other idea, pls do not hesitate to write it.

Sorry to bother you again. I don't think it's a good idea to remove the pkgver() function. Without it, the version is hardcoded in the PKGBUILD, no matter what version the actual code has. As stated [here](https://wiki.archlinux.org/index.php/VCS_package_guidelines#The_pkgver.28.29_function):

```
It is recommended to have following version format: RELEASE.rREVISION where REVISION is a monotonically increasing number that uniquely identifies the source tree (VCS revisions do this).
```

This could be achieved with:
```
pkgver() {
  cd "$pkgname"
  git describe --long --tags | sed 's/\([^-]*-g\)/r\1/;s/-/./g;s/^v//g'
}
```
At this time this results in following version:
```
0.10.r5.gbde20cb
```

Aside from that you should suffix the package name with `-git` unless it fetches a specific release.

You could also just have it checkout the latest tag and increase the `pkgver` variable at each release (or just fetch the release without git and provide a checksum). In that case, the `-git` suffix is not needed.

I haven't tested myself, but from the problem description, it sounds like #1412 is a CPU problem, not a memory problem.

If you're instantiating/tearing down a new `KafkaProducer` instance every time, that is a huge waste of resources, and would certainly use additional memory.

For most scenarios, you should create a single long-lived instance per Python program and then use that single instance to send all messages to Kafka... you can send to multiple topics with the same instance, it's effectively just a database connection. The only exception is if you need separate instances with different configs that are can only be set on instantiation.

worker.py
```python
import asyncio
from aio_pika import connect, IncomingMessage, Message, DeliveryMode


channel = None


async def on_message(message):
    with message.process():
        print("Received message %r" % message.body)
        new_message = Message(b'New message', delivery_mode=DeliveryMode.PERSISTENT)
        await channel.default_exchange.publish(new_message, routing_key='task_queue2')


async def main():
    global channel
    connection = await connect("amqp://guest:guest@127.0.0.1/")
    channel = await connection.channel()
    queue = await channel.declare_queue('task_queue', durable=True)
    await channel.declare_queue('task_queue2', durable=True)
    queue.consume(on_message)


loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.create_task(main())
loop.run_forever()
```

If you're not yet fully invested in `nflgame`, might I suggest having a look at `nfldb`.
@burntsushi wrote a cookbook recipe that can easily be re-purposed for your use there.

```python
import nfldb

def inside(field, stat):
    """
    inside takes a field position and a statistical category, and
    returns a predicate. The predicate takes a play and returns true if
    and only if that play ends inside the field position determined by
    adding the statistical category to the start of the play. (e.g.,
    play yardline + receiving yards determines the field position where
    CJ was tackled.)
    """
    cutoff = nfldb.FieldPosition.from_str(field)
    return lambda play: play.yardline + getattr(play, stat) >= cutoff

db = nfldb.connect()
q = nfldb.Query(db)
q.game(season_year=2012, season_type='Regular')
q.play(punting_tot=1, punting_touchback=0)

plays = filter(inside('OPP 10', 'punting_yds'), q.as_plays())

for p in plays:
  print p
```

Hi! There isn't a specific method in lasio for reading a whole folder of files at once, because it really depends on what you want to do with the files. Do you want to do something independently with each LAS file in turn, or do you want them all stored in memory at once?

For the first example:

```python
import glob
import lasio

for filename in glob.glob('*.las'):
    las = lasio.read(filename)
```

To store them all in memory, you might for example store them in a dictionary:

```python
import glob
import lasio

las_data = {}
for filename in glob.glob('*.las'):
    las = lasio.read(filename)
    las_data[filename] = las
```

These might help too:

- [Listing files in a directory](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/3207219/how-do-i-list-all-files-of-a-directory)
- [Using dictionaries to store things](http://www.compciv.org/guides/python/fundamentals/dictionaries-overview/)

@meet-bhagdev and myself did a bit of playing around with pyodbc and this is what we found out, at least as related to SQL Server :-) Hopefully it can help.

For more background on prepared statements in SQL Server you can take a look at [my first reply](https://github.com/Microsoft/mssql-jdbc/issues/166#issuecomment-284490675) to the JDBC issue that @gordthompson mentioned. 

After testing pyodbc a bit with SQL Server it seems like pyodbc never re-uses a server-side prepared statement handle (please correct if wrong). I.e. you either do cursor.execute("my SQL code", ..) with or without params. There seems to be no way to re-issue the same "prepared statement" without sending the SQL code to the server again (something like cursor.executePrepared(statementHandle, <params>) if that is not what cusor.executeMany does...).

Right now the following seems to be the case:
1. cursor.execute("SELECT ...") - Execute without parameters. Calls ODBC SQLExecDirect which for SQL Server will generate a language event/SQL batch call. 
2. cursor.execute("SELECT ... WHERE col = ?", 42) - Execute with parameters. Calls ODBC SQLPrepare to bind parameters followed by SQLExecute at which time the SQL Server driver will generate a TDS RPC request to sp_prepexec in turn preparing a handle that can be re-used on the server. 
3. On connection close ODBC SQLFreeStmt seems to be called to unprepare the last prepared statement (sp_unprepare for SQL Server).

Here is my SQL Server trace:
![pyodbc_screenshot](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1648796/23973586/06b97378-0994-11e7-9041-fcc0975d1f2b.png)

Looking at these results it seems like:
1. Is great :-)
2. Should be changed to call SQLExecDirect instead of SQLExecute, at least for SQL Server. You still call SQLPrepare first since that should not issue a call to the server and I think that is required for parameter binding as mentioned earlier in the thread. 
3. If other RDBMSs require the use of SQLPrepare, SQLFreeStmt would need to be called after each statement.

Thanks!
/Tobias

We have three options for the save(). They are:

### Option A.

    idf.save() # -> Save in the native format of the host machine
        # if you are on a unix machine, the line endings would be unix
        # if you are on a windows machine, the line endings would be DOS
    idf.save(lineendings='windows') # -> forces the lineendings to be windows


### Option B.

    idf.save() # -> save in the existing file format
        # if the file format is unix, the line endings continue to be unix
        # if the file format is windows, the line endings continue to be windows
    idf.save(lineendings='windows') # -> forces the lineendings to be windows


### Option C.

    idf.save() # -> always save in windows format
    idf.save(lineendings='windows') # -> redundant but still works


## Pros and Cons of each option

### Option A.

    idf.save() # -> Save in the native format of the host machine
        # if you are on a unix machine, the line endings would be unix
        # if you are on a windows machine, the line endings would be DOS
    idf.save(lineendings='windows') # -> forces the lineendings to be windows

**Pros:**
- follows pythonic behavior. This is how python code would normally behave
- follows zen of python -> Explicit is better than implicit

**Cons:**
- you have to force line endings to be `windows` when you are on a unix machine


### Option B.

    idf.save() # -> save in the existing file format
        # if the file format is unix, the line endings continue to be unix
        # if the file format is windows, the line endings continue to be windows
    idf.save(lineendings='windows') # -> forces the lineendings to be windows

This is how the software BBedit and TextWrangler work.

**Pros:**
- This is how a lot of text editing software work
- This is expected behavior in the GUI environment

**Cons:**
- not obviously pythonic
- plain vanilla python code does not behave this way



### Option C.

    idf.save() # -> always save in windows format
    idf.save(lineendings='windows') # -> redundant but still works

**Pros:**
- will always work with present version of IDFEditor

**Cons:**
- This is a special case for IDFEditor
- IDFEditor may change and this may no longer be a special case
- breaks Zen of python -> Special cases aren't special enough to break the rules


**Option A** is already implemented in the `master` branch, but not documented

` Except when propagating a received Forwarded-NPDU message, the "B/IP Address of Originating Device" field in

communicates. This is required so that responding devices on the remote subnet may communicate with the originating
device. Received Forwarded-NPDU messages are propagated as-is to foreign devices and to the local IP subnet as
defined in J.4.5 `

I am referring to the way `get_end_time` is used in the [fast_fluidsynth gist](https://gist.github.com/craffel/50a422f431350f0b3b09) and [midi-dataset.feature_extraction.fast_fluidsynth](https://github.com/craffel/midi-dataset/blob/master/feature_extraction.py). The two cases are essentially the same code and the code is used for cropping the synthesized midi audio. 

Even though the fast_fluidsynth code isn't part of the official codebase for pretty midi, I've ended up using it in the `example/align_midi.py` procedure as a workaround for issues with fluidsynth.py. 

Yes, after looking at how you've used get_end_time() in `pretty_midi` I admit that I was considering a too-narrow scope for the method. There are good use cases for the method as is. And after some further research I saw that you use pretty_midi.instrument.fluidsynth to crop according to note, pitch-bend, and control-change events and ignore meta events such as lyrics and tempo changes. I initially overlooked this code because I am using fast_fluidsynth instead.

This whole rant started with me trying to figure out why that one midi file had so much silent audio. Now I understand how get_end_time works and how it fits into the larger picture, so I'm okay with how things turned out.

@felixSchl Js2Py is slow. This is both because JavaScript is much faster than python and because some overheads due to translation (about 5 times slower than manual translation). Also a suggestion - it is much easier to call functions JS functions. Just use .to_python method to convert them to python-like object: 

`run = docopt_purs.var.to_python().PS['Docopt.FFI'].run`

OR 
`run = docopt_purs.var.get(u'PS').get('Docopt.FFI').get('run').to_python()`

Both methods are equivalent. This makes things much easier, all the conversion from python to JS is done automatically and you can simply call run like normal python function:

```
run("Usage: foo bar", {
    'dontExit': True,
    'argv': [s for s in sys.argv[1:]],
    'env': {}})
```

Hi,

Excel doesn't display strings as values for charts and the values in the `rows` data are strings. From the output of your program:

```
('rows: ', [['mkt01', '23'], ['mkt02', '32'], ['mkt03', '40'], 
            ['mkt04', '47'], ['mkt05', '30']])
Done writing excel.
('categories: ', ['mkt01', 'mkt02', 'mkt03', 'mkt04', 'mkt05'])
('values: ', [23, 32, 40, 47, 30])
('XlsxWriter Version: ', '0.7.7')
```
As you can see the value in `rows` are strings. The values in values are numbers. If you convert the `rows` values to numbers you will see a properly populated chart.

If you want XlsxWriter to convert number-like strings to numbers automatically you use the `strings_to_numbers` [constructor option](https://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.org/workbook.html):

> **strings_to_numbers**: Enable the worksheet.write() method to convert strings to numbers, where possible, using float() in order to avoid an Excel warning about “Numbers Stored as Text”. The default is False. To enable this option use:

>      workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook(filename, {'strings_to_numbers': True})

Regards,

John

No, this feature can be implemented with all job stores

Workaround: 
You can add another field "group" or "group_name" in each job which has some value by default. 

Adding a task:

task = scheduler.add_job(group="user defined group",
                                         ....)

in Redis or other NoSQL, an entry will be created:

keyvalue pair: apscheduler.groups:group_%s <--> [list of job_ids]

Retrieving job using group_name:

tasks = scheduler.get_jobs(group="user defined group", limit=30) # By default limit will be -1

Redis Query behind:

job_ids_list = redis_store.lrange("user defined group",
                                 limit)

As you retrieve job_ids_list just lookup in particular jobstore and get job
for job_id in job_ids_list:
     list_jobs.append(scheduler.get_job(job_id))

> The idea is that, for a given intent, the slots have their own prompts,
> help etc attached to them. So if you trigger an intent with 3 slots say,

### Single vs in-directory

Keeping a cache file in each `sys.path` directory is easier to work with conceptually - if the directory has different paths because it's accessed over NFS or bind-mounted into docker containers, the same cache works for all of them. If you delete the directory, you delete its cache. And in my case, the speed penalty over a single cache is minimal.

The performance of a single cache may be more important if you have a lot of entries on sys.path (eggs). It also allows you to create a cache for directories that you cannot write to, such as system directories, and for entry points from zip files (eggs again) without needing a special case.

### Format options

- SQLite seems like a good solution, but it has a major achilles heel: NFS. Databases on NFS mounts get corrupted often enough that I don't want to rely on SQLite anywhere that the file might be stored in NFS.
- Something like [cdb](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cdb_(software)) would work for this: create a new cache and replace the old one atomically, never updating an existing file. But we'd need to either parse the format in Python, or ship an extension module to parse it efficiently.
- I've gone for JSON, because Python already comes with a JSON parser implemented in C, so it should be cheap to read. That it's a familiar, human-readable format is a bonus.

To be honest, I'm not too keen on throwing errors because it is effectively the same as the current behaviour (just with a more meaningful error message). However, it gets in the way of usability because you'd have to trace down all dangling symlinks and delete them before you could send a directory. Also, there may potentially be good reasons why you don't want to (or can't) alter the source directory.

Therefore I think I'd prefer warnings over errors (and avoiding to copy over dangling symlinks).

For convenience, there could even be a switch to allow receiving dangling symlinks (if you're sending files to yourself), but that's likely overkill.

This requirement tripped me up. Consider the following code:

```python
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class Wrapper:
    value = field()
```

I assumed I could use it like:

```python
instance = Wrapper(1)
```

But because the `value = field()` declaration does not have a type annotation, `Wrapper(1)` raises a TypeError.

```
TypeError: __init__() takes 1 positional argument but 2 were given
```

To get it to work, I have to import `typing` and use `Any`:

```python
from typing import Any

@dataclass
class Wrapper:
    value: Any = field()
```

which adds unneeded verbosity to unannotated code. It would be nice if unannotated attributes on a `dataclass` decorated declaration that are instances of `dataclasses.Field` would be treated as fields on the data class.

A separate block is one option, and probably preferable to having to specify it with each rule.

You can have it implicitly, but you have to make clear how it works in term of the language concepts. Define in the documentation explicitly when a rule is a terminal rule, and define all other rules as non-terminal rules (if you have only 2 kinds of rules). I don't remember seeing this, but I may have missed it. With assignment say it only works for non-terminal rules. If a user tries it anyway, point to "only allowed in a non-terminal rule" so the user hopefully finds the discussion.

Having a terminal rule (or even a terminal section), doesn't mean you cannot have terminal literals in non-terminal rules. Simplest is perhaps to define them as a rewrite to an unused terminal rule name that gets added to the terminal section. (The implementation may not actually do that, but it's an implementation detail, much like your smart LR compression that is fully hidden for me. The implementation does however have to keep the illusion consistent, docs are leading rather than implementation.)

As long as both forms are fully equivalent, a user is free to pick whatever he/she prefers, until he/she wants to use a feature that is not defined for one kind of rules (at that point a rewrite to the more basic form is required).

Ok.

If you run the code without a proxy, you shouldn't get an error. awslimitchecker knows to ignore that error from AWS; the problem you're seeing is because your proxy is changing the error that AWS returns, so the code doesn't know how to handle it.

Your options, as far as I can see, are:
1. don't use a proxy.
2. have someone fix the proxy so it returns the same error that AWS does, instead of a different one.
3. don't run awslimitchecker for the services that don't exist in specific regions. This is a bit of a pain in a shell script, but could easily be done via the Python API.

-Jason

Huh, looks like `ffmpeg -filters` gives useful information:
```
 T.C volume            A->A       Change input volume.
 ... volumedetect      A->A       Detect audio volume.
 ... aevalsrc          |->A       Generate an audio signal generated by an expression.
 ... anoisesrc         |->A       Generate a noise audio signal.
 ... anullsrc          |->A       Null audio source, return empty audio frames.
 ... sine              |->A       Generate sine wave audio signal.
 ... anullsink         A->|       Do absolutely nothing with the input audio.
 ... alphaextract      V->N       Extract an alpha channel as a grayscale image component.
 ... alphamerge        VV->V      Copy the luma value of the second input into the alpha channel of the first input.
 TS. atadenoise        V->V       Apply an Adaptive Temporal Averaging Denoiser.
 TS. avgblur           V->V       Apply Average Blur filter.
 T.. bbox              V->V       Compute bounding box for each frame.
 ... bench             V->V       Benchmark part of a filtergraph.
 T.. bitplanenoise     V->V       Measure bit plane noise.
 ... blackdetect       V->V       Detect video intervals that are (almost) black.
 ... blackframe        V->V       Detect frames that are (almost) black.
 TS. blend             VV->V      Blend two video frames into each other.
 T.. boxblur           V->V       Blur the input.
 TS. bwdif             V->V       Deinterlace the input image.
```
It even includes input/output specifications to tell how many input/output streams each filter expects. Should be able to parse this and auto-generate filter functions with simple docstrings.

Instead of joining arguments with `:`, pass each argument as its own string:
`ffmpeg.filter_(stream, 'crop', 'iw*.63', 'ih*.63', 'iw-(iw*.64)', 'ih-(ih*.833)')`

I'll try to think of a way to make this more obvious, (or maybe have a warning when `:` is included in a filter arg).

For this particular example you can do it by creating the output stream manually, i.e.

```python
i1 = ffmpeg.input("audio.mp4")
i2 = ffmpeg.input("video.mp4")
out = ffmpeg.nodes.OutputNode([i1, i2], "output", kwargs={"filename": "output.mp4", "c:v": "libx264", "c:a": "aac"}).stream()
print(" ".join(out.compile()))
```
which prints out
```
ffmpeg -i audio.mp4 -i video.mp4 -map [0] -map [1] -c:a aac -c:v libx264 output.mp4
```
(I haven't tested it and I'm not 100% sure, but the map arguments are quite useless and are implicit in your command line)

I don't think this is the best solution though (it's an ugly workaround actually), and it should be part of what #45 does. We'll need to think of how to implement it in a more user friendly manner.
Maybe something like

```python
out = ffmpeg.output(stream, "output.mp4", normal_kwarg="test", kwargs={"kwarg-with-non-python-characters:a": "test"})
```
for every function in the API that creates a new KwargsReprNode.

In order to properly support foreign keys and other relations in the
revision form template, the model is loaded from the revision database and
saved back into the database. At the end of the view, the database
transaction is rolled back.

This requires that the model's save() method is called, which
correspondingly calls any post_save signals. If any of the post_save
signals have side-effects that take place outside the database, they will
persist outside of the revision preview.

This behaviour is definitely awkward. Solutions and discussion to work
around it would be appreciated!

So, by introspecting Version.content_type.model_class()._meta.fields, you
can see what fields correspond to foreign keys and m2m relations.

You can get the version data for that referenced model by looking in
Version.revision.version_set.all(), which is a queryset of all other models
in the revision. With knowledge of the primary key value, and the
referenced model class from the introspected field, you can find the
Version of the referenced model in the queryset.

```python

```python
import ipyvolume.pylab as p3

```
This will create js/data/style.json, which is required, since in embedded mode, the defaults will not be passed. This will also make is possible to change the theme outside the notebook in the future.

Hi Bruno,

False alarm, nothing to fix here on the python-redmine side.

You're getting this error because of the ``time_entry.save()`` call which is not needed, because ``redmine.time_entry.create()`` is a self-sufficient method which creates and returns created resource in one step, i.e, when you do:

```python
time_entry = redmine.time_entry.create(
    spent_on=datetime.now(),
    issue_id=1,
    hours=1,
    activity_id=9,
    comments=None
)
```

python-redmine calls Redmine API, creates the time entry resource and then you get the created resource object in time_entry variable. After that when you do ``time_entry.save()`` python-redmine throws the exception that no fields were set for creating/updating the resource which is exactly the truth.

``.save()`` method should be used when you're creating/updating resource in several steps, e.g.:

```python
time_entry = redmine.time_entry.new()
time_entry.spent_on=datetime.now()
time_entry.issue_id=1,
time_entry.hours=1,
time_entry.activity_id=9,
time_entry.comments=None
time_entry.save()
```
Now implemented in https://github.com/peterbrittain/asciimatics/commit/d4fa28a48a1876d4219dd6785e34570caa274eed

So this is just a work around for the main issue:

`CreateAPITestCaseMixin.test_create()` requires self.object to have been created via which uses 
`BaseRESTAPITestCase.get_object()`, however CreateAPITestCaseMixin.create_data is used to create a second object. So creating 2 objects where only one is required. This has implications for `models.OneToOneField` and possibly other use cases as well (see initial post in this issue).

Possible solution:

- Implement `CreateAPITestCaseMixin.setUp()` to call BaseRESTAPITestCase.setUp() with a `no_create `parameter so it doesn't implement this in BaseRESTAPITestCase.setUp() `self.object = self.get_object(self.get_factory_class())`

- `CreateAPITestCaseMixin` to have a .model property that CreateAPITestCaseMixin.test_create() calls  here instead of self.object         ```
created = self.object.__class__.objects.get(
            **{self.lookup_field: self.get_lookup_from_response(response.data)})
```

Could make backwards compatible by looking for `CreateAPITestCaseMixin.model` and then using that or self.object if no `CreateAPITestCaseMixin.model`.

@djpimp2010 here is an example that works, using a "green thread", or greenlet, with eventlet as @miguelgrinberg suggested. This would work much the same with gevent too... It's very similar to your code, except we're not using the stdlib Thread module.

I believe an example like this is also included in the flask-socketio library.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

import eventlet
eventlet.monkey_patch()

from flask import Flask
from flask_socketio import SocketIO

app = Flask(__name__)
socket = SocketIO(app, logger=True, engineio_logger=True)

def bg_emit():
    socket.emit('bg_emit', dict(foo='bar'))

def listen():
    while True:
        bg_emit()
        eventlet.sleep(5)

eventlet.spawn(listen)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    socket.run(app, host='127.0.0.1')
```

emitting event "bg_emit" to all [/]
emitting event "bg_emit" to all [/]
emitting event "bg_emit" to all [/]
```

I've given this some thought, and it really doesn't make very much sense to me.

There's really no way to determine if an object has a "custom" order at the adminsortable admin class level. Sorting the objects is determined by a positive integer. As such the methods that affect the order would fail if the specified field isn't an integer.

There's nothing special about the position of `ordering` values that could be used to determine if that is the "custom" ordering. it's just another field.

You can always override the ordering of objects at the queryset level regardless of the order specified on the model, which is really the best option I can see, since ordering fields don't override the previous value.

Example:

    class Person(models.Model):

        fn = models.CharField(max_length=50)
        ln = models.CharField(max_length=50)

        class Meta:
            ordering = ['ln', 'fn']

will instruct the class to order objects by the last name, ascending AND the first name ascending, not first name if it comes after the last name.

So specifying a class with an ordering of `['the_order', '-date']` would sort by the order, and the date, which is not what I think you're expecting to happen.

At the moment, TensorFlow clusters (without TFoS) are statically configured, i.e. each node must know the host:ports of all other nodes (i.e. cluster_info) at startup time.  This is why we have a `reserve` step, and why we disable Spark dynamic allocation via: `--conf spark.dynamicAllocation.enabled=false` to ensure that one executor == one TF node.

So if a Spark executor fails for any reason, the TF cluster is essentially broken.  Note that this is no different than if you had manually set up the TF cluster (w/o TFoS) on several machines and one of the TF nodes dies.  That said, TF would attempt recover IF you were able to restart the TF node on the failed host on the same port.  Unfortunately, Spark/YARN does not attempt to restart a failed executor on the same host (and it's difficult to re-reserve the same port even if we could), so the only recovery at the moment is to restart the job from the last saved TF checkpoint.

@smarthi Why did you close the issue?

This could break existing code. Sometimes the resulting array of indices is subject to another operation. Then the result can become different if the dtype of the index array changes. Or it can make an error as some opertions can be applied to only floating point arrays.
```python
nd.topk(nd.arange(10), k=2).mean()
```
Currently the result is `[8.5]`, but the result is `[8]` if the dtype is integer. 
```python
nd.gemm2(nd.topk(nd.arange(10), k=2), x)
```
Currently this is allowed, but it'll cause an error if the dtype changes to integer.

`multinomial` has a similar but opposite issue. It returns an index array of integer type, but it would be convenient to have floating point array sometimes (#10970). I think that it would be better if we can make `multinomial` and `topk` consistent.

    """
    return 'CURRENT_TIMESTAMP'


@compiles(sql_utcnow, 'mysql')
def mysql_sql_utcnow(element, compiler, **kw):
    """MySQL returns now as localtime, so we convert to UTC.

    Warning:
        This must be executed and cannot be used as a
        column server_default value.
    """
    return "CONVERT_TZ(CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,@@session.time_zone,'+00:00')"


@compiles(sql_utcnow, 'sqlite')
def sqlite_sql_utcnow(element, compiler, **kw):
    return "DATETIME('NOW')"


@compiles(sql_utcnow, 'mssql')
def mssql_sql_utcnow(element, compiler, **kw):
    """MS SQL provides a function for the UTC datetime."""
    return 'GETUTCDATE()'
```

Usage then goes from:

```python
created_dttm = Column(UtcDateTime, default=func.now())
```

to

```python
created_dttm = Column(UtcDateTime, default=timezone.sql_utcnow())
```

In my project, I'm using **impyla** as one of several database drivers which could be accessed via DB API 2.0. Users should be able to upload/insert small datasets/tables (probably 100 rows max) into the database. Right now this will produce hundreds of files in HDFS while this PR allows to avoid it.

According to PEP 249:

> [`.executemany(...)`](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249/#executemany)
> ...
> Modules are free to implement this method using multiple calls to the `.execute()` method **or by using array operations to have the database process the sequence as a whole in one call**.

[Impala docs](http://www.cloudera.com/content/cloudera/en/documentation/cloudera-impala/v2-0-x/topics/impala_insert.html#values_unique_1) also give following recommendations on `INSERT...VALUES`:

> If you do run `INSERT ... VALUES` operations to load data into a staging table as one stage in an ETL pipeline, **include multiple row values if possible within each `VALUES` clause**, and use a separate database to make cleanup easier if the operation does produce many tiny files.

> Do we really want to have the same coding style for everyone?

Having worked on many different projects over many years of writing & designing software I do see a value in keeping code consistent.

Code is communication.  It's less about communicating with the computer than it is about communicating with other humans.  The more consistent a codebase is, the easier it is to read, understand, and refactor.

You will never find a format that makes everyone happy because each of us has our own style, but when you're collaborating with others, you have to let go of these little things to focus on the bigger things.  (For me the comma at the beginning of the line in the initializer or jamming things together without spaces drives me nuts - but I'm totally on board with prefixing class members with *m*!)

Like @LegalizeAdulthood I am not a significant contributor, so ultimately the stye decision should come from the main contributors, but I do think some basics (spacing, line endings, any naming conventions, preferring initializers, should build without warnings) should be defined for any project.

Or you could just rewrite in Go and use [gofmt](https://blog.golang.org/go-fmt-your-code) :-)

Hi, there are currently no plans to add this to boto3.  It looks like this functionality is available as a cfn stack (https://docs.aws.amazon.com/solutions/latest/instance-scheduler/deployment.html) so there's not really much boto3 would be doing here except launching the stack for you.

These higher level commands can be made up of several lower level commands, sometimes of different types, so there is not one set of response data, or even one type of response to return in all cases, and wouldn't make sense to try and merge them or show it all, so to keep it clean we don't return any of the individual responses.

@josefkorbel I would not recommend making API calls to test your code for the reasons you noted. Instead, I would recommend checking out the [stubber interface](https://botocore.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/stubber.html). This is handy because you provide the responses up front, and the stubber will validate the provided response and return it through the client method call. You can also do assertions on what was called. This ultimately should allow you to test code that interacts with SQS without actually making an API call. Let us know if you have any questions.

## New Dependency

1. `pipenv install foo==X.Y.Z` to install a new dependency (optionally providing --dev flag where appropriate).
2. Commit Pipfile and Pipfile.lock.
3. In other environments run `pipenv sync`.

## Existing Dependency

1. Edit Pipfile version directly or use `pipenv update foo==X.Y.Z`.
2. Run `pipenv lock`.
3. Commit Pipfile and Pipfile.lock.
4. In other environments run `pipenv sync`.

Basically, `RequestTraversalAgent.request` calls `Agent.request`.  `Agent.request` gets some protocols connected up with `iosim.connect`.  The last step of `iosim.connect` is to pump the iosim.  `pump` unconditionally calls `resumeProducing` on **any** registered producer.  It should not call `resumeProducing` on streaming (aka push) producers - only non-streaming (aka pull) producers.

So this is an iosim bug.  Yet.. even with it fixed, `RequestTraversalAgent` provides rather unsatisfactory results when a body is supplied, never delivering the bytes written by the producer (or never until manual iosim pumping is performed).

If RF=1 with two brokers and one broker dies, some partitions will have no replica and thus will have no eligible leader broker. This is why Kafka returns -1 in this case, and why PyKafka currently does not support it. To run in such cases (when the number of available brokers is less than the replication factor), a client like PyKafka would have to make it clear that some messages were not being consumed due to lack of a leader for their partition.

@gth828r To answer your question:

> is the priority of implementing the new consumer API once you are comfortable with it's stability?

The answer is "yes and no". Our first priority is to make sure that pykafka can work with the 0.9.0 broker and do balanced consuming the same way as it did in 0.8.2 (via the "old" consumer API and Zookeeper).

Once that is stable and working, pykafka will have a single source that works equally well with 0.8.2 and 0.9.0, which is the immediate goal.

We will *then* begin exploring the new 0.9.0 consumer APIs. What we aren't sure about right now is whether switching pykafka to those APIs will require breaking compatibility with 0.8.2, and how all of that relates to the parallel work going on in the librdkafka community.

The set of "fetch_locks" on owned partitions (all of which are taken for a reset_offsets call) can be acquired in a variety of different orderings in the reset_offsets(...) call depending on its arguments (and the unreliability of item ordering between different python dicts)

If your call is intersecting with another call to reset_offsets() I believe it can deadlock.  Another call to this could be happening asynchronously, for example, in the error handler for an OffsetOutOfRangeError and possibly other places.

A rule of thumb here is that resources should be acquired in the same order by anyone who wants more than one.  This prevents for example: "thread A holding lock 1 while waiting for lock 2" while "thread B holding lock 2 waiting for lock 1".

This is also likely why a calling reset_offsets() one by one does not cause a deadlock (only takes one lock)

I suggest creating a stable sorted ordering for this iteration: 
https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/master/pykafka/simpleconsumer.py#L513

Generally here, all this fetch_lock stuff is also rather scary as any sort of exit from these functions by exception will leave all sorts of dangling acquired locks...

If you change the dtype, you change the range in which you function, so if the max value was 90% of the old range, then clip to 90% of the new range.  If interpolation overshoots, we can have a ``clip=True`` flag, on by default.

> It's not ideal, but since (afaik) PNG and JPEG don't support Boolean format this issue may be beyond our ability to address.

FYI: PNG supports a bit depth of 1, which could be used when saving a boolean image.  But then what data type and values would you expect to get when reading such an image?  I recently had to deal with this issue in scipy (https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/5289).  When reading a 1-bit PNG file, `scipy.misc.imread` now returns an array with dtype `np.uint8` containing the values 0 and 255.  (That's what `skimage.io.imread` does, too--at least with the 'pil' plugin.)  This behavior is consistent with how it handles 2-bit and 4-bit files, where the values are scaled up to the 8-bit range.  For example, when reading a 2-bit file, the array that is returned contains the values 0, 85, 170 and 255.  *That* behavior is inherited from Pillow.  (It is also the behavior suggested in the PNG specification.)

What determines which parts of the image will be _inside_ and _outside_ is the initialization of the contour. It is expected that the user will provide a _good_ initialization with a large proportion of the inside area in the `foreground` part of the level-set and vice versa. The following code shows how to specify the index by the edge modes.
`reflect` mode uses the inner index to interpolate the outside of the image.
I think there is no reason to use the inner index for the outside.
`edge` mode always uses closer index than `reflect` mode.
```
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import skimage.transform

a = np.arange(10, dtype=np.float64).reshape(1, 10)
modes = ['constant', 'edge', 'reflect']
outputs = {}
for mode in modes:
    outputs[mode] = skimage.transform.rescale(a, (1, 1000), mode=mode).squeeze()
for mode, output in outputs.items():
    plt.plot(output, label=mode)
plt.legend(loc=2)
plt.savefig('index.png')
```
![index](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1255726/37806347-ca6277de-2e82-11e8-8978-09711dce4d7f.png)
In #1514, `reflect` was recommended because it is similar to the behavior of OpenCV' `resize`, but it is same as `edge` actually.
This is another reason I recommend `edge` mode.
```
import cv2
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

a = np.arange(10, dtype=np.float64).reshape(1, 10)
plt.plot(cv2.resize(a, (10000, 1)).squeeze(), label='OpenCV')
plt.legend(loc=2)
plt.savefig('opencv.png')
```
![opencv](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1255726/37806340-c23f5888-2e82-11e8-814e-ee911b2ae227.png)

@dset0x WRT `REWORDS`, we sometimes don't want to only reword a message, but change the meaning completely, because the feature might have been changed N times and even dropped before the release is made.  (See the [use case discussed above](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/issues/2856#issuecomment-77568892).)  In this case, we'd be not only "rewording" previous commits, but rather "amending" its features, as it were.

@jirikuncar WRT `AMENDS`, I like it better than `REWORDS`.  Though I'm not sure whether the git analogy plays in our favour here.  Technically, we are not "amending" last commit and its message, we are "superseding" what was done in perhaps N previous commits in the past; hence `SUPERSEDE` may be perhaps clearer.  Anyway `AMENDS` seems fine as fell.

> Isn't it the same thing? If you change "validate with custom schema validator class that replaces required, min*, max* rules with dummy checks" by "schema with reduced constraints" in the workflow @jirikuncar suggested, the result should be the same.

The end-result, i.e. the JSON being saved to DB, yes is the same. However, with the custom validator you have saved a JSON which doesn't validate according to the JSON Schema spec, whereas when you have a schema with reduced constraints you have saved a JSON which validates according to JSON Schema spec.

My main concern here is that I think Invenio-Records should be a small clean module with a clear simple API, and that we will make a bit more dirty by allowing to diverge from JSON Schema spec. In the end, Invenio-Records is a crucial module, and if we slowly allow feature-creep we will end up with Invenio 1's patchwork (exaggerated of course so don't take it literal :-). Invenio-Records is perhaps our most core module so we should think very hard about features we add into.

@jirikuncar: Regarding [the discussion about try/except and begin_nested blocks](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-accounts/pull/108#discussion-diff-51550263) that's now on an "outdated diff":

- You were right about the `IntegrityError` being thrown by `db.session.commit()`, I misread the stack trace.
- The exit function of `begin_nested` does not catch the `IntegrityError` thrown when the `unique=True` constraint of `SessionActivity.sid_s` is violated. 

The following code snippet results in an unhandled `IntegrityError`:
```python
from invenio_db import db as _db
from invenio_accounts.models import SessionActivity

session_activity = SessionActivity(user_id=u'1', sid_s=u'stringval')
with _db.session.begin_nested():
    _db.session.add(session_activity)
    _db.session.add(session_activity)   
```

Placing the `with ...begin_nested()` block inside a `try` block catches the exception:
```python
from invenio_db import db as _db
from invenio_accounts.models import SessionActivity

session_activity = SessionActivity(user_id=u'1', sid_s=u'stringval')
try:
    with _db.session.begin_nested():
        _db.session.add(session_activity)
        _db.session.add(session_activity)
except IntegrityError:
    _db.session.rollback()
```

I've published binaries and a configuration guide here: https://github.com/saucecontrol/Brotli-IIS

> For funcions which return 1 on success and 0 on failure, as well as other booleans (like the is_last argument to BrotliEncoderWriteMetaBlock), please use a bool (or _Bool if you prefer) intsead of an int. Using bool helps make the code more readable and reduce documentation lookups.

A counter-argument: I don't think the `bool` concept really provides a stable, portable ABI in C.

I was experimenting with the new mod_brotli for the Apache web server, which makes use of the libbrotli shared library. Unfortunately, the module and the library disagreed on the definition of `BROTLI_BOOL`, because the module was compiled using C89 and the library was not. This led to a situation where the library returned "false" but the module interpreted that value as "true", which broke everything.

In my opinion, an ABI that changes depending on the C standard in use by the compiler is not going to be portable enough for widespread use. I agree with the idea that a boolean concept is more documentation-friendly than `int`, but the current implementation causes problems. (And I humbly submit that `int` is still the most "correct", idiomatic type for booleans in a portable C ABI.)

Compare the following:

```
import fire
from __future__ import print_function

lamb = lambda: print(123)

class CallableClass:
    def __call__(self):

fire.Fire(lamb)
fire.Fire(CallableClass) # note `inspect.getsourcefile` throws when this is used in the interpreter
fire.Fire(CallableClass())

>     if file_mode is None: file_mode = not sys.stdout.isatty()
> AttributeError: 'Logger' object has no attribute 'isatty'

Workaround we found is to set 
```
import sys
sys.stdout.isatty = lambda : False
sys.stdout.encoding = None
```
in Zeppelin notebook before we call  SW API.

I’m starting to think more about how to extend AtmosphericPSF to accommodate evaluating multiple PSFs through a single atmosphere at different field angles.  Things to abstract include:

Atmosphere
PhaseScreen
AtmosphericPSF

and potentially something to handle both Fraunhofer and Fresnel propagation, which might just be two different methods of `Atmosphere`.

For a frozen flow phase screen atmosphere, multiple PSFs are relatively straight forward, since it doesn’t matter whether the inner loop is the exposure time integration or the loop over multiple PSFs.  For non-frozen flow, the inner loop needs to be over the multiple PSFs, since otherwise, by the time you’ve finished integrating the first PSF and are ready for the second, the atmosphere has irreversibly changed.  This is clearly difficult to accommodate in the draw-galaxies-into-subimages-one-at-a-time approach we (or at least I) typically use.  Any suggestions?  Should we ditch non-frozen flow phase screens?  Or maybe retain them but with the understanding that PSFs evaluate sequentially in time and not concurrently?

Finally, I'm debating two potential ways to connect `Atmosphere` with `AtmosphericPSF`:

```
 atm = Atmosphere(r0, lam, etc.)
psf1 = atm.getPSF(x1, y1)
psf2 = atm.getPSF(x2, y2)
```

and

```
atm = Atmosphere(r0, lam, etc.)
psf1 = AtmosphericPSF(atm, x1, y1)
psf2 = AtmosphericPSF(atm, x2, y2)
```

I'm leaning toward the second option since it's closer to the way `RealGalaxy` and `RealGalaxyCatalog` interact, though beyond that I don't have a strong preference.  Thoughts?

> I'm curious what @rmjarvis has to say about this too.

This is more a question of coding style than anything else.  Putting the functionality into ChromaticObject wouldn't be wrong, but in my opinion, it seems like the more object oriented way to do this is how Rachel did it.

The basic question is probably whether the interpolation paradigm is potentially appropriate for all kinds of Chromatic objects?  Or are there just some Chromatic objects that should be treated as an InterpolatedChromaticObject while others shouldn't?

If it's the former, then the interpolation option should just be an option for all Chromatic classes, and the functionality should be moved to the main ChromaticObject base class.

If it's the latter, then I think it is better to leave the InterpolatedChromaticObject class as a level in the class hierarchy that specific child classes can derive from to get that functionality.

My guess is that it is the latter, since I don't think the Chromatic class (simple object * sed) would benefit from any of the interpolation stuff.  But maybe not.  Maybe even that one should have the interpolation option.

I refer to neuromorpho.org<http://neuromorpho.org> (http://neuromorpho.org/myfaq.jsp, "What is SWC format?"): "The index for parent compartments are always less than child compartments." This follows original definition by Cannon et al, 1998 ( http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0165027098000910). Thus the continuity requirement seems to be excessive.

Whether the period unit should be an attribute of the `formula` or of the `variable` is a very good, tricky, and interesting question 👍 . Let's try to think this through and to get a consensus.

I've read that "a variable may be called on any period", but this statement is a design choice and needs to be discussed. Does a `rsa` exist for `2013-2014` ? Its sum exist, its average too, but the `rsa` itself ?

@benjello, among the variables you know, would you say that **the formula to compute them** have a natural period unit, or that the **variable themselves** have one. For the one I know in the social prestations world, it is more the variable which is well defined for a given period.

The concrete behaviour question behind this is : should we control the period units of users input ? When I set the input of my simulation, can I declare a `rsa` for `2015`, or should I get an error if I try ? Can I declare an `irpp` for `2015-04` ?

> Can you give an order of magnitude of the difference observed ? It's really interesting to quantify on concrete experiments the benefits of vectorial computing :)

I'd say that it depends on the context: the cost of the computations we'd like to avoid (ie `allegement_fillon`) versus the time iteration is done in Python. I didn't want to test it explicitely because it would have depended on the cost of the formula computation.

What I did test is the time of iteration in Python over a 1e5 array, and it's > 10 seconds. I don't mention about 1e6 ... never really finished the test, I had to interrupt it.

So that's why I advocate for a `switch` helper which outputs an expression equivalent to the multiplication syntax, and rely on the cache of the Core.

Some additional notes:

Currently, only support inplaced operations for certain nodes with the following conditions:
(1) has one and only one input, whose dim() should be the same as the output.
(2) no need for the original input value in the backward gradient calculation, thus is ok to optimize out.
(3) no special gradient manipulations thus backward will not be broken.
Therefore, only selecting certain nodes: Reshape, Identity (READ); Rectify, LogisticSigmoid, SoftSign, Tanh, Dropout* (WRITE).

------------------------------------------------------

Since inplaced operations directly share memories, there indeed might be certain silent failures which will result in wrong calculations without any warnings. Thus, currently the rules for inplaced nodes are relatively strict. (Most of them are checked in `ComputationGraph::set_inplace_for_new_node` by DYNET_ARG_CHECK).

Here are the failing situations checked (default action is throwing runtime_error), wonder if there are anything remiss or anything breaking the procedure, might need some help for this.

-- failing situation 1: values cannot be restored or used for it is already changed by WRITE-inplace nodes.

    # failing situation 1.1: cannot restore value for revert
    import dynet as dy
    x = dy.zeroes(100)
    dy.cg_checkpoint()
    y = dy.tanh(x, True)
    y.value()
    dy.cg_revert()
    
    # failing situation 1.2: cannot restore value for get_value
    import dynet as dy
    x = dy.zeroes(100)
    y = dy.tanh(x, True)
    y.value()
    print(x.value())
    
    # failing situation 1.3: cannot use the node whose value has been WRITE-borrowed
    import dynet as dy
    x = dy.zeroes(100)
    y = dy.tanh(x, True)
    z = x + 1

-- failing situation 2: cannot add inplace nodes due to the current exclusive property of WRITE-inplace. (This is relatively strict for the purpose of a simpler implementation: the principle is READ is shareable while WRITE is exclusive.)

    # failing situation 2.1: *-inplace + WRITE-inplace
    import dynet as dy
    x = dy.zeroes(100)
    y = dy.tanh(x, True)
    y2 = dy.tanh(y, True)
    
    # failing situation 2.2: used node (by plain nodes or READ-inplace nodes) + WRITE-inplace
    import dynet as dy
    x = dy.zeroes(100)
    z = x + 1
    y = dy.tanh(x, True)

(In fact, some WRITE+WRITE operations might be possible to implement, like `tanh+dropout` which is typical for hidden layers, which could be extended but need to carefully specify the rules . However, most of them seems to unfeasible because we need the intermediate values for gradient calculation.)

-- ok situation: multiple READ-inplaces are allowed since they are not changing the original values.

    import dynet as dy
    x = dy.zeroes(100)
    x1 = dy.reshape(x, (10,10), True)
    x2 = dy.reshape(x, (10,10), True)
    x11 = dy.reshape(x1, (100,), True)
    for z in [x,x1,x2,x11]:
        print(z.dim())

(Currently no supports for NoBackprop and FlipGradient, whose gradients need special manipulations and thus are not that easy to be correctly shared.)

------------------------------------------------------

For simplicity, currently make all the inplaced nodes non-auto-batchable (return 0 for autobatch-sig). Since it seems that auto-batch need the inputs in contagious memory, which usually cannot be guaranteed. Thus this simple strategy might be ok? since the purpose of inplace operations is saving memories.

------------------------------------------------------

Inplace operations should only be a way of optimization for memories and will never bring any other changes. Thus two programs with the same codes except the inplace operations should obtain exact the same results. Tests will be needed on real tasks, expecting the same results and models; I'll plan to test this soon.

Hi Thomas,

I don't have ready access to the Debian BTS, so I'm going to update here. 

I don't 100% know what's going on here, but I think it is an issue with traceback2 which we started to exhibit after 55278e0e4a935f1d8c468a4d29a8133088806790 when we started to depend on it.

https://github.com/testing-cabal/traceback2/blob/master/traceback2/__init__.py#L449 assumes that if an exception has a `__cause__`, then the cause has a traceback. Clearly this assumption does not always hold. 

`__cause__` is normally set when an exception is re-raised using the new Python 3 `raise from` syntax. Python 3 correctly takes care of setting `__traceback__` on `__cause__`. 

However, it turns out that Django has special code for setting `__cause__` on database exceptions:

> As per [PEP-3134](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-3134/), a `__cause__` attribute is set with the original
> (underlying) database exception, allowing access to any additional
> information provided. (Note that this attribute is available under
> both Python 2 and Python 3, although [PEP-3134](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-3134/) normally only applies
> to Python 3.)

(from [exceptions.txt](https://github.com/django/django/blob/53ccffdb8c8e47a4d4304df453d8c79a9be295ab/docs/ref/exceptions.txt#L195-L199), some markup edits)

I would suggest that:

1. Django should be a little bit better behaved, and at least set `__cause__.__traceback__` to None
2. traceback2 should be a bit more tolerant of poorly behaved exceptions, and wrap the access of `__traceback__` in a `getattr`

Hope this helps,
jml

> I may be missing something here, but couldn't the implementation of the call into the engine be masked, deferring the optimizations for behind-the-scenes work?:

`result = yield rust(value)` is still a "python calls rust" situation... so the coroutine would not actually be doing in anything useful with the `yield`. In this PR the `@rule` yields to the rust code which sends back the value. Again, really not certain that it buys us less time under the GIL. But it's recursion safe at least?

Adding an explicit encoding to every `open` seems like the right thing to do in some sense, but doesn't feel very pythonic somehow? Part of the joy of python is how easy it is to mess with files... 

In Python 3.7 we can actually force UTF-8,  by settings the env var `PYTHONUTF8=1` or adding the cmd line flag `-X utf8` to the cpython invocation. See https://docs.python.org/3/using/cmdline.html#envvar-PYTHONUTF8. 

So perhaps we should require Python 3.7 for running Pants once we're fully on py3, and until then make this check more strict so that it fails for any preferred encoding that isn't UTF-8?

@benjyw i implemented this locally at my company by wrapping the node plugin.  It works for us because the output_dir is a special directory inside the resolve directory that doesn't exist when resolve is cached and the compile step caches just that output_dir

so specifically, we have output_dir set as public/dist
resolve creates:
```
ls .pants.d/resolve/node/current/src.javascript.fe-dist/current/
app                     npm-shrinkwrap.json     tsconfig.json           webpack.config.js
node_modules            package.json            webpack.config.dev.js   webpack.config.prod.js
```
compile creates:
```
ls .pants.d/resolve/node/current/src.javascript.fe-dist/current/public/dist/
0.da71a5560fc2b54eeb57.bundle.js                16.e2fb50a36b9c1cb40007.bundle.js               7.da71a5560fc2b54eeb57.bundle.js
0.da71a5560fc2b54eeb57.bundle.js.map            16.e2fb50a36b9c1cb40007.bundle.js.map           7.da71a5560fc2b54eeb57.bundle.js.map
0.e2fb50a36b9c1cb40007.bundle.js                17.e2fb50a36b9c1cb40007.bundle.js               7.e2fb50a36b9c1cb40007.bundle.js
```
and the compile cache just caches public/dist.

so yeah, this wouldn't work for everyone and is super weird.  i could try to merge noderesolve and nodecompile into one step (compile) but that feels weird.  how do other language libraries solve this problem?  I know java uses ~/.ivy2 or something but node needs all the dependencies in a local path to compile.  i think go is similar?

So, there is a clean way to post reviews, but last I checked, an errorprone way to merge them.

Assuming you "feather" branches, ie: for 3 stacked changes `a`, `b`, `c` where `upstream` is the pantsbuild/pants remote and `origin` is your fork remote - you have local branches: `upstream/master`<-`a`, `origin/a`<-`b` and `origin/b`<-`c`, then when you push, say `c` to `origin` and click the yellow ribbon to create a PR in the github UI:

![createpr](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/157586/30867208-8f8a768c-a298-11e7-9167-88cfc381dc9d.png)
 
You'll get a default PR based against `upstream/master`, which you don't want:

![defaultbase](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/157586/30867230-a6af6b10-a298-11e7-969e-dc60f0094b43.png)

So you adjust the base dropdown to your fork, and then select the `b` branch as your base, diff is now right:

![adjustedbase](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/157586/30867299-cc686dde-a298-11e7-970d-4cdfe111ef20.png)

So this is all well and good, if, while `c` review is in-flight, you make `b` changes - as long as those are pushed to `origin`, the `c` PR diff updates as expected.

The issue comes on the merge side. Last I tried this in maybe March 2017, you had to merge `c` - not `b` or `a`, and merging `c` would pull in `a` and `b` to master because of the feathering. The problems here are:

1. You (or worse, the committer reviewer) must remember to only click merge for the leaf PR, `c` in this case. Easy to forget this.
2. We'd need to give up our current squash merge setting to preserve each PR as a separate commit.

And, not sure about this one, but pretty sure, ogithub won't be smart

Sorry, answering my own issue here, but after a lot of poking and prodding I think I've figured out what's going on here. 

So the documentation for pantsbuild for python_binary -> compatibility (https://pantsbuild.github.io/build_dictionary.html#python_binary) states the following:

```
compatibility either a string or list of strings that represents interpreter compatibility for this target, using the Requirement-style format, e.g. 'CPython>=3', or just ['>=2.7','<3'] for requirements agnostic to interpreter class.
```

Specifically the bit about using `['>=2.7', '<3']` to target 2.7 interpretors. I ran the pex file using:

`PEX_VERBOSE=1 dist/apps.pex`

to see if there was any information there, and immediately saw that the pex file was linking against python 2.6 on my mac instead of the 2.7 version which the application was built for. 

Here's a snippet:
```
pex: Using prescribed entry point: apps.apps:main
pex: Found site-library: /Library/Python/2.6/site-packages
pex: Found site extra: /System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.6/lib/python2.6/../../Extras/lib/python
pex: Tainted path element: /System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.6/Extras/lib/python
pex: Tainted path element: /Library/Python/2.6/site-packages
pex: Tainted path element: /System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.6/Extras/lib/python/PyObjC
pex: Scrubbing from site-packages: /System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.6/Extras/lib/python
pex: Scrubbing from site-packages: /Library/Python/2.6/site-packages
pex: Scrubbing from site-packages: /System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.6/Extras/lib/python/PyObjC
```

After removing `'<3'` from the compatibility file the pex file properly targets 2.7 and runs perfectly fine.

Not sure if this is a change required on the pantsbuild docs or if there's an issue with parsing the compatibility property of python_binary and python_library or if I misinterpreted the docs for the compatibility property.

I'm closing this because the test failures are nondeterministic and not associated with a specific Python version. Reducing the number of Python versions we run the test suite against would likely reduce the number of erroneous test failures, but would not reduce the number to zero. We should determine the cause of the flakiness.

(The CI failures are timeouts on the Python tests, so presumably not your fault.)

Failing hard at runtime seems unhelpful, as that could happen in production if the feature is only used in rare cases (and words triggering those cases aren't in the test data) - if you're going to fail hard, I think it has to be at compile time (either snowball compile time, or generate rust code which won't compile, assuming rust is a compiled language).

Perhaps it's more helpful to try to implement them though - at least then when/if we have a compliance test suite, we won't have to find someone to implement them from scratch.

FYI, another language feature not used by any current stemmer is `$` on a string variable (which is what the `copyfrom()` mechanism is for in most of the backends - the C backend handles this differently though).
